ideas.State of nature as war of all against al:
The **state of nature** as a "war of all against all" is the founding thought experiment of Hobbes's political philosophy — a bleak picture of human life without government that grounds his whole case for the state. To discover the rational basis of political authority, Hobbes strips away civil society and asks what human existence would be like in its absence, with no common power to keep people in awe. His answer is grim.

By nature, Hobbes argues, human beings are roughly **equal** in strength and cunning (even the weakest can kill the strongest by stealth or alliance), and they compete for the same scarce goods. This equality of ability breeds equality of hope, and thus competition, which combined with mutual **diffidence** (fear) and the desire for **glory** produces perpetual conflict. In the absence of any overarching authority to enforce agreements and provide security, no one can trust anyone else, and each must, rationally, strike first and grab what they can. The result is the "*bellum omnium contra omnes*" — the war of all against all.

Hobbes describes this condition in his most famous passage: with no industry, agriculture, arts, or society, there is "continual fear, and danger of violent death; and the life of man, solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short." Crucially, in this state there is no justice or injustice, no right or wrong, for these presuppose a common law — "where there is no common power, there is no law." This intolerable condition, Hobbes argues, gives every rational person overwhelming reason to seek peace by erecting a sovereign — the launching point for his theory of the social contract.
ideas.Social contract and absolute sovereignty:
Hobbes's theory of the **social contract** and **absolute sovereignty** is his solution to the terror of the state of nature, and one of the most influential (and controversial) arguments in political philosophy. If the natural condition of humanity is a war of all against all, in which life is "nasty, brutish, and short," then reason itself — through what Hobbes calls the "laws of nature" — dictates that we seek **peace**. And peace is possible only if everyone lays down their unlimited natural right to all things and submits to a common power strong enough to enforce order.

This is achieved by a **covenant**: each person agrees with every other to transfer their right of self-government to a **sovereign** — a single person or assembly — on the condition that everyone else does the same. By this mutual authorization, a multitude of individuals is unified into a single artificial body, the **commonwealth** or "**Leviathan**," a "Mortal God" to which we owe our peace and defense. The sovereign is authorized to act for all; his acts are, in effect, the acts of each subject.

Hobbes insists that this sovereign power must be **absolute** and **undivided**. To limit or divide sovereignty — between king and parliament, or church and state — is to invite the very conflict the contract was meant to end; a divided sovereignty collapses back toward civil war. The sovereign is not himself party to the contract and so cannot breach it; subjects retain only the right of self-preservation. Though absolutist in its conclusions, Hobbes's grounding of political authority in the consent and rational self-interest of individuals made him a founder of modern social-contract theory and liberalism's great antagonist and inspiration.
ideas.Materialism and mechanistic psychology:
Hobbes was one of the first thoroughgoing **materialists** of the modern age, and his mechanistic account of mind and human nature underlies his entire philosophy, including his politics. Inspired by the new science of Galileo and the geometry of his time, Hobbes held that **everything that exists is body in motion** — there are no immaterial souls, spirits, or incorporeal substances. The universe is matter obeying mechanical laws, and human beings are no exception: we are complex, self-moving machines. "For what is the heart, but a spring; and the nerves, but so many strings; and the joints, but so many wheels?"

From this materialism flows a **mechanistic psychology**. All human thought begins in **sensation**, which Hobbes explains as the internal motion produced in us by the pressure of external bodies on our sense organs. Imagination is "decaying sense," the lingering motion after the object is gone; memory, dreams, and thought are all variations of these internal motions. Even the **passions** are reduced to motions: desire (or "appetite") is an internal motion *toward* an object, and aversion a motion *away* from it. What we call "good" is simply the object of our appetite, and "evil" the object of our aversion — there are no objective values in nature, only these psychological motions.

This deterministic, materialist picture of human beings as machines driven by appetites and aversions in ceaseless pursuit of self-preservation and power ("a perpetual and restless desire of power after power, that ceaseth only in death") is the naturalistic foundation on which Hobbes builds his account of the state of nature and the necessity of the sovereign. It made him a pioneer of modern scientific naturalism about the human mind.

works.Leviathan:
*Leviathan* (1651) is Hobbes's masterpiece and one of the greatest works of political philosophy ever written — the founding text of modern social-contract theory and of the modern conception of the state. Written during the upheaval of the English Civil War, it is a systematic argument, built from first principles, for the necessity of a strong, unified sovereign power as the only sure remedy for the chaos of political disorder. Its title, drawn from the biblical sea-monster, names the great artificial being — the commonwealth — that Hobbes constructs.

The book builds methodically from the ground up. Beginning with a **materialist** account of human beings as machines of sensation, appetite, and aversion ("Of Man"), Hobbes derives the psychology of self-interested individuals ceaselessly seeking power and self-preservation. From this he deduces the terrifying **state of nature**, a "war of all against all" in which life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short." Reason, through the "laws of nature," then dictates the escape: individuals covenant together to erect a **sovereign** ("Of Commonwealth"), transferring their natural rights to an absolute, undivided power that alone can guarantee peace and security.

The later, often neglected halves of the book ("Of a Christian Commonwealth" and "Of the Kingdom of Darkness") turn to religion, arguing forcefully that ecclesiastical power must be subordinate to the civil sovereign, so that no divided allegiance can reignite civil war. Combining rigorous logic, vivid prose, and a stark vision of human nature, *Leviathan* framed the central questions of modern political thought — authority, obligation, rights, and legitimacy — and every subsequent contract theorist, from Locke to Rousseau to Rawls, wrote in its shadow.
works.De Cive:
*De Cive* ("On the Citizen," 1642) is Hobbes's first full and systematic statement of his political philosophy, published nearly a decade before *Leviathan* and containing, in more compact and rigorous form, many of the doctrines for which he became famous. Hobbes conceived it as the third and final part of a grand philosophical system — *De Corpore* (on body), *De Homine* (on man), and *De Cive* (on the citizen) — but the urgency of England's political crisis led him to compose and publish this concluding part first.

The work lays out Hobbes's core political argument with great clarity. It analyzes the human condition first in the **state of nature** — the natural liberty of individuals which, given human equality, competition, and fear, collapses into a state of war and universal insecurity. It then examines the transition to civil society through **covenant** and the transfer of right to a sovereign power ("Dominion"). Finally, it treats **religion** and the proper relation of the church to civil authority, arguing for the subordination of ecclesiastical to sovereign power.

Written in Latin for a learned European audience, *De Cive* presents Hobbes's political science as a demonstrative discipline modeled on geometry, deriving political conclusions from clear definitions and premises about human nature. Many of its central theses — natural equality, the war of all against all, the necessity of an absolute and undivided sovereign, and the primacy of self-preservation — reappear, enriched and elaborated, in *Leviathan*. Influential across the Continent (where it was read before *Leviathan*), *De Cive* is essential to understanding the development and structure of Hobbes's political thought.
works.De Corpore:
*De Corpore* ("On Body," 1655) is the foundational, most purely philosophical work of Hobbes's projected three-part system, laying out the **metaphysics**, logic, and natural philosophy on which his account of man and citizen was to rest. Where *Leviathan* and *De Cive* made him famous as a political thinker, *De Corpore* reveals the deeper materialist and scientific framework underlying all his thought.

Its guiding doctrine is a rigorous **materialism**: philosophy, for Hobbes, is the study of **bodies** and their motions, and of the causes and effects that can be rationally demonstrated from them. He excludes theology and anything immaterial from philosophy proper, since only bodies can be causes. The work covers logic and method (including his nominalist theory that reasoning is a kind of "computation," the adding and subtracting of names), first philosophy (space, time, body, cause, and motion), geometry, and the general principles of physics — all conceived as a science of matter in motion.

Hobbes here articulates the mechanistic worldview inspired by Galileo: a universe consisting solely of bodies moving according to necessary causal laws, in which all change is motion and all explanation is mechanical. This provides the metaphysical grounding for his mechanistic psychology (sensation and passion as internal motions) and, ultimately, for his political science. Though his forays into mathematics led him into a notorious and losing controversy with the mathematician John Wallis over his attempts to square the circle, *De Corpore* remains a key document of seventeenth-century materialism and of Hobbes's ambition to build a complete, unified philosophy — from body, to man, to the commonwealth — on scientific foundations.
